body: What does stride mean in this context? Because step doesn't equal chunk_size + stride_left, it is kind of hard to interpret what exactly stride_left or stride_right represent.
author: literate-goggles

body:
If you calculate the step in this way, the actual overlap with the left chunk would be 2 * (stride_left + stride_right).
Consider, for example, chunk_len = 15, stride_left = 3, stride_right = 3, and step = 15-3-3 = 9.
0 chunk: start=0, end=15,
1 chunk: start=9, end=24,
2 chunk: start=18, end=33

For 1 chunk, the overlap with the 0 chunk is 6, and the overlap with the 2 chunk is 6, totaling 12 of overlap, but the intended overlap was 3 with the left chunk and 3 with the right chunk, totaling 5 of overlap.

body:
There's no `top_k` with a nice API on `numpy` so I would stay away from `native`-> `np` -> `native` or have the ugly `topk` version in `np`.

Overall this looks to me as the least worst of all codes.

Do you know a clean `np` version of `topk` (would indeed make things cleaner)
author: Narsil

body: I think one can do a top_k using `np.argpartition` and/or `np.argsort`, do you mean that there's no `np.topk` when you say there's no clean version?
author: LysandreJik

body:
I mean  `np.argsort/np.argpartition` is not as clean as `torch.top_k`.

~~`top_k` can be done in `O(n + k log(k))` where sorting is `O(n + k log(k))` with numpy apparently~~ Same complexity.

https://stackoverflow.com/questions/6910641/how-do-i-get-indices-of-n-maximum-values-in-a-numpy-array/23734295#23734295

```python
ind = np.argpartition(a, -top_k)[-top_k:]
indices = ind[np.argsort(a[ind])]
values = a[indices]
```
is also pretty unreadable. 

I don't think having a single `if framework == "tf"` in the decoding is that bad.

body:
It is breaking in some sense yes.

Calls like `AutomaticSpeechRecognitionPipeline(feature_extractor, model, tokenizer)` will break.
But, the regular pipeline has a call signature `(model, tokenizer, feature_extractor)`, so this makes it a little bit consistent.
It also makes the parent class responsible of doing `self.feature_extractor = feature_extractor` (so less risk of future discrepancy between pipelines). It's definitely something that should probably be a separate PR, and make sure ALL PR henceforth have a correct call signature. We could also use something like `MyPipeline(*, model. tokenizer, feature_extractor)` which would disallow purely position based arguments, which would make everything simpler to maintain IMO with reducing confusion for users too: https://www.vegardstikbakke.com/python-keyword-only/

I will revisit the PR to exclude this from the current PR, it's definitely not good just putting that here, I would rather make a sweep at all classes and make a solid (tested) decision about parameter flow. (I think the current thing, is just something that was written before the rewrite of pipelines and just happened to be that way, not a conscious decision.
author: Narsil

body:
It is captured by `postprocess`, however I don't think it's needed really.

Most likely some local experiment that I ended committing by error.
FYI, the goal was to enable the caller to fuse the chunks themselves, in the context of a stateless pipeline that would still be usable by a live microphone. Since the pipeline is stateless, it couldn't know the last used token in order to set it in the striding area (so it would get properly discarded when doing CTC decoding).

But as I found out, a purely stateless pipeline, incurs way to much network overhead to be viable (The current script settings would mean 4Mo/s bandwidth, vs 64ko with a stateful connection, so stateful connection it is, and so we can ignore `raw_ctc` altogether :)).

body:
I think that for the normalization we have to add these 3 operations: 
- `NFC`, it is a unicode normalization that is used by `ftfy.fix_text ` (see corresponding code [here](https://github.com/rspeer/python-ftfy/blob/master/ftfy/__init__.py#L131-L140)) and that is quite common to add
- `Replace(Regex(r"\s+"), " ")`, as it is a normalization that is used by the `whitespace_clean` method defined in the slow tokenizer (see corresponding code [here](https://github.com/huggingface/transformers/blob/v4.15.0/src/transformers/models/clip/tokenization_clip.py#L95)) and that is quite common to add
- `Lowercase` as the text is always lowercase in the slow version (but see my question 6. in the introductory post)
author: SaulLu

body: I added a test to be able to test cases which according to me should not be tokenized differently between the slow version and the fast version of the tokenizer
author: SaulLu

body:
For the pre-tokenization step, I reused the regex used by the original tokenizer to pre-tokenize the text before applying BPE (corresponding [code](https://github.com/huggingface/transformers/blob/v4.15.0/src/transformers/models/clip/tokenization_clip.py#L318-322) in the slow version). I just removed the special tokens from this regex as they will already be isolated by the fact that they are added tokens.

As explained in my point 4. above, I also forced `add_prefix_space` to `False`.

I'm not entirely sure we can actually merge this PR without adding a whole new class:
- there are existing `ClipTokenizerFast` files in the Hub that will become incompatible with Transformers
- we can say "let's replace them all" but then they will stop working with older versions of Transformers.

Maybe the versioning of the tokenizer.json files can help here (e.g. you can upload several tokenzier.json files for the same repo, one for older versions and one named tokenizer_vxxx.json only used starting vxxx).

author: patrickvonplaten

body:
Yes, I hesitated back&forth. Adding a decoder, means adding a bunch of code everywhere to automatically load it up etc.. For a single pretty niche (currently) use case.
Adding it as tokenizer, is defendable as you said, and makes things simpler for all utils. It's not however really a tokenizer (since it takes `logits` not `input_ids`). The good thing, is that for the pipeline it should roughly be seen as an implementation detail, not really something users should depend on  (if it's `tokenizer`).

I don't like adding `kwargs` without careful thought (pipelines are already a kwarg mess, adding on top is something worth at least a pause).

- Isn't there a way to make the decoder itself have the kwargs ? That means better ownership of information. (overridding kwargs means the user now has to declare himself `decoder = pytctc.Decode(..., **kwargs); pipe = pipeline(..., decoder=decoder)` and at least who eats those arguments is extra clear.
- Adding them to the raw pipeline is like the `generate_kwargs`, not every argument can be changed in the future, and user has no idea if the argument was correctly passed. (Misnamed argument gets silently discarded)
- Other option if `decoder` doesn't own it is to have `pipeline(..., decoder_kwargs={..})`. This is cleaner as it adds only 1 kwargs and it's unlikely to be miss attributed. However, those are not "owned" by the decoder itself, meaning the behavior is pipeline specific. Going that route the arguments to pipelines are going to get extremely long (they already are).

body:
```suggestion
            Ratio of MLP hidden dimensionality to embedding dimensionality.
```
`mlp_ratio` is something that is used in a lot of timm models, as well as the original implementation of Swin Transformer.

Note that for most existing models in the library, we don't use `mlp_ratio`, but we rather define an `intermediate_size`, which determines the dimensionality of the intermediate MLPs (e.g., ViT's config has an `intermediate_size` attribute set to 3072).

This is a bit of an inconsistency. Are we ok with proceeding with `mlp_ratio`?

cc @LysandreJik @sgugger

author: LysandreJik

body:
Hello @LysandreJik. The errors do provide some details:

For example, if Ninja isn't installed, there is a RuntimeError in the stacktrace - `RuntimeError: Ninja is required to load C++ extensions`.  

As another test, I tried to use the kernels with the incorrect PyTorch version on a A100 GPU, and I see the following warning: `NVIDIA A100-PCIE-40GB with CUDA capability sm_80 is not compatible with the current PyTorch installation. The current PyTorch install supports CUDA capabilities sm_37 sm_50 sm_60 sm_61 sm_70 sm_75 compute_37.
If you want to use the NVIDIA A100-PCIE-40GB GPU with PyTorch, please check the instructions at https://pytorch.org/get-started/locally/`.

So, I think this should be enough to inform the users what to do if the compilation isn't successful. 

Btw, I moved the function call to load kernels from `YosoSelfAttention` to `YosoEncoder`, because it seems unnecessary to compile the kernels every time a self-attention class is initiated. Instead, I feel it's more efficient to load it only once - in the encoder.

body:
Once again, your comment led to insightful conclusions -- digging deeper, I found that the `past_key_values` output of the decoder is different on PT and TF (TF's is a tuple with the encoder's hidden states and past key values, and PT's is only the later). I had adapted the code from PT, so my `past_key_values` was missing the encoder outputs, and I had to pass them explicitly in order to work.

I've reverted this change and made TFSpeech2Text's `past_key_values` match the expected TF shape. An updated TF generate would probably have prevented this error 🤔

body: I don't really understand why this would cause an issue in TF<=>PT exactly - what was the issue? BTW, we always wrap the static sinusoidal encodings in a `nn.Parameter()` or `nn.Embedding(...)`, E.g. Vit's positional embeddings, DistilBERT's position embeddings. Why should we do it differently here?
author: patrickvonplaten

body: But maybe it's totally fine. What do you think @LysandreJik @sgugger ?
author: patrickvonplaten

body:
> what was the issue?

This is the only model that I could find that may change the size of the embedding layer in the forward call ([here](https://github.com/huggingface/transformers/blob/master/src/transformers/models/speech_to_text/modeling_speech_to_text.py#L188)). The TF version for other embeddings allocates these named model parameters in [`build()`](https://www.tensorflow.org/api_docs/python/tf/keras/layers/Layer#build), which is expected to happen before the first `call()`. Since `nn.Parameter()` is mostly a wrapper to add the variable to the model's list of parameters (AFAIK), then dropping it means we don't need a named parameter in TF, which means we can simplify the code and avoid solving the resizing issue.
author: gante

body: Mmm, but dropping it also removes it from the checkpoint, no? This might prevent proper loading/saving AFAICT.
author: sgugger

body:
Facebook checkpoints do not have it and they load correctly in TF and PT regardless of this change. I'm assuming that, for the same reason, we exclude errors when loading a checkpoint in PT and this variable doesn't exist, as it is in `_keys_to_ignore_on_load_missing` ([here](https://github.com/huggingface/transformers/blob/master/src/transformers/models/speech_to_text/modeling_speech_to_text.py#L1239)).

The issue that prompted me to do this change was uncovered in the equivalence tests, where we try to load in TF a PT model that does not come from a checkpoint ([here](https://github.com/huggingface/transformers/blob/master/tests/test_modeling_tf_common.py#L362)). We also have this var in `_keys_to_ignore_on_save`, but the test attempts to load a PT model in TF without saving it first  -- i.e., having the var in `nn.Parameter()` breaks the test because it is not excluded, contrarily to the checkpoints.
author: gante

body: Technically, someone who fine-tuned (or just saved and reshared) a Speech2Text model will have this weight in the checkpoint. I don't know how it's used exactly, so I don't know if it's breaking to ignore it (we would have to put it in the `_keys_to_ignore_on_load_unexpected`).

body:
```suggestion
So far we have used the [`Trainer`] to run the experiments but a more flexible alternative to that approach is to use [`accelerate`](https://huggingface.co/docs/accelerate/). With 🤗 Accelerate you have full control over the training loop and can essentially write the loop in pure PyTorch with some minor modifications. In turn it allows you to easily scale across different infrastructures such as CPUs, GPUs, TPUs, or distributed multi-GPU setups without changing any code. Let's see what it takes to implement all of the above tweaks in accelerate. We can still use the [`TrainingArguments`] to wrap the training settings:
```

body:
```suggestion
If the model does not fit on a single GPU with all the mentioned tricks there are still more methods we can apply although life starts to get a bit more complicated. This usually involves some form of pipeline or tensor parallelism where the model itself is distributed across several GPUs. One can also make use of DeepSpeed which implements some of these parallelism strategies along with some more optimization to reduce the memory footprint such as partitioning the optimizer states. You can read more about this in the ["Model Parallelism" section](parallelism). 
```

body:
Thanks for working on it, @lvwerra. We need more of similar content.

That's said I think a better solution would be to rewrite the existing documents and not add a new one that has more than 50% overlap with the existing doc, which will make it more confusing to the user and more difficult to maintain for us, as many things will now be duplicated if this PR is merged as is.

Indeed the original performance doc has been grown into scalability+performance and parallelism is no longer parallelism but actually it's the scalability doc, that's specifically there to deal with OOM.

I think we need 2 docs:

1. scalability
2. performance

and move/combine this PR's doc with the scalability sections of the the current performance doc - removing the huge overlap that this PR proposes.

and for the performance doc leave only software/hardware bits that talk about speeding things up, and minimally talking about memory usage.

Bottom line, IMHO it'd be good to brainstorm how to do that in the most harmonious way before creating a new document.

body:
```suggestion
## How to finetune with OVH cloud

For a more detailed guide on setting up OVHcloud please watch this video: TODO

### Creating an OVHCloud account
*TIP*: If you haven't created a project on OVHcloud yet, make sure you've received your GPU voucher code *beforehand*, 
so that you can skip entering the credit card information.
1. If you're a US citizen, create an account via [OVHcloud.CA](https://ovhcloud.ca/). 
If you're from anywhere else in the world, create an account via [OVHcloud.COM](https://ovhcloud.com/).
2. Once logged in, click `Public Cloud` from the top menu and then click `Create your first OVH Public Cloud project`. 
Then enter a project name (e.g. "huggingface"), enter your voucher code, and click `Continue` -> `Create my project`.
*Note: if you see a request for credit card details during the last step, and you can't skip it, then your voucher code 
is invalid. Please report it to the [#ovh-support](https://discord.gg/p4qqDV3M) channel on Discord.*

### Setting up an AI notebook
1. Go to the `Public Cloud` page and select `Project Management` -> `Users & Roles` from the menu on the left. 
2. Click `+ Add user`. Write a user description (e.g. `AI Trainer`), and select an `AI Training Operator` user role. 
Click `Confirm`.
3. Write down the *username* and *password* (at the top of the screen) somewhere. They will be needed during step 7.
4. Select `AI & Machine Learning` -> `AI Training` from the menu on the left. 
Click `+ Launch a new job` on the AI Training page.
5. On the `Launch a new job` page:
   * In `1. Choose a region` select a region closest to you.
   * In `2. Enter the Docker image` select `Custom image` -> `baaastijn/ovh_huggingface`.
   * You can skip steps `3.` and `4.` if you will be using the Hugging Face Hub to store the models after training.
   * In `5. Configure your job` select **1** `GPU`.
   * Validate the info and Create the job.
6. On the `AI Training Jobs` screen wait until the job's status changes from `Pending` to `Running`.
7. Click `HTTP Access` and log in with the AI training user you've created earlier. 
Once logged in, you can close the page and click `HTTP Access` to launch a JupyterLab notebook.
8. Awesome, now you have a free GPU-enabled Jupyter instance!

**Note**: If you're an experienced Docker user, feel free to create a custom docker image with all of the needed packages 
like the one in step 5. The Dockerfile for it is available here: 
[baaastijn/Dockerimages](https://github.com/baaastijn/Dockerimages/tree/main/Hugginface_challenge_speech).
Once you've built your own image, push it to https://hub.docker.com/ and select it during the OVHcloud job creation.
```

body:
```suggestion
With so many different Transformer architectures, it can be challenging to create one for your checkpoint. As a part of 🤗 Transformers core philosophy to make the library easy, simple and flexible to use, the `AutoClasses` automatically infer and load the correct architecture given a checkpoint. The `from_pretrained` method lets you quickly load a pretrained model for any architecture so you don't have to devote time and resources to train a model from scratch. Producing this type of checkpoint-agnostic code means if your code works for one checkpoint, it will work with another checkpoint (as long as it was trained for a similar task) even if the architecture is different.
```

body:
```suggestion

TINY_MODEL = "hf-internal-testing/tiny-random-distilbert"

def test_set_progress_bar_enabled():
    with patch("tqdm.auto.tqdm") as mock_tqdm:
        set_progress_bar_enabled(True)
        _ = AutoModel.from_pretrained(TINY_MODEL, force_download=True)
        mock_tqdm.assert_called()

        mock_tqdm.reset_mock()

        set_progress_bar_enabled(False)
        _ = AutoModel.from_pretrained(TINY_MODEL, force_download=True)
```

we want to have tests finish as fast as possible. We have a set of tiny models under https://huggingface.co/hf-internal-testing that can be used for functional tests and they are usually a few MBs-large, instead of much larger models. So I proposed to use that here.

author: stas00

body:
As it is now, toggling `set_progress_bar_enabled` won't change the behavior of the progress bar in the `Trainer` class; it only concerns downloads. `Trainer` uses `tqdm.auto.tqdm`.

https://github.com/huggingface/transformers/blob/226e74b610bd8d30c2faba627f245d693ced7b55/src/transformers/trainer.py#L33

For `set_progress_bar_enabled` also take affect in `Trainer`, the above import line should be replaced with `from .utils.tqdm_utils import tqdm`. This is in line with my previous inquiry:

> Should toggling `set_progress_bar_enabled` affect the entire API, for instance the behavior of the Trainer class? This would mean replacing all occurrences of from `tqdm.auto import tqdm` with `from .utils.tqdm_utils import tqdm`.

To my ears, the docs edit misleadingly suggests otherwise. What do you think?

Here is how I think about it: `http_get` is the function that invokes tqdm, and not its callers. Therefore a user tracing the tqdm call will find that `http_get` calls `tqdm`, and read the doc that explains how to control this behavior.

If this note is removed now the user will have no idea how to control this behavior, unless they think to go to the logging doc and read it there, which they may or may not think of it, since tqdm doesn't use logging.

body:
Padding works, it's handled by the batching mecanism. All I mentionned here is why we need so much information (`logits` might get padded while `stride` no).

We *can* make it work, relatively trivially for left padding.

```python
if self.feature_extractor.padding_side == "left":
    left = logits.shape[1] - total_n + left_n
    right = logits.shaoe[1] - total_n + right_n
else:
   left = left_n
   right_n = total_n - right_n
```
Just thought this was overly complex since left padding doesn't seem likely here.

body:
Not a huge fan of the name - it is in the end some kind of length penalty to me so could we maybe change it to something like:

```
ExponentialDecayLengthPenalty
```
?
author: patrickvonplaten

body: Think it would be cleaner to just have a single input tuple here `exponential_decay_length_penalty`. What do you think @Narsil ?
author: patrickvonplaten

body:
I do agree a tuple is better.

The reason is if a user adds only 1 argument, then the current code would fail (since one would be None and the other have a real value).
So having a tuple forces users to set both at once, making it less likely to crash/fail.
I don't think `config` is allowed to have tuples be defined though (I don't know tbh). Is it ?

Another thing though, is that this doesn't take into account already existing tokens (either in encode-decoder or in decoder-only). IMHO, this makes this argument harder to use, since you have to offset it with the start_ids, which you might not have access to (for instance pipeline users). `max_length` as the same issue and `max_new_tokens` is there to be agnostic to the initial length. 

If we could make `length_regulation_start` become expressed in generated tokens instead of overall tokens ,it would make a nicer api IMO.
author: Narsil

body: You won't believe how i struggled finding a proper name for the logitsprocessor. I like ExponentialDecayLengthPenalty, will change it accordingly.
author: kevinpl07

body:
Tuple is fine for me. any ideas on an expressive name for the tuple? 

The pipeline arguments makes sense to me, I would change it in the same fashion as max_new_tokens. Do you agree with this change @patrickvonplaten?
author: kevinpl07

body:
`config` is allowed to have tuples - tuples are immutable so that's totally fine for configs in my opinion. I'm also to have length regulation more dependent on the number of generated tokens as suggested by @Narsil! `max_new_tokens` is better than `max_length` in this regard. 

Regarding the name, I don't really mind that much as long as its docstring is good and the name closely resembles the logit processor class name.

body:
Good point! 

I believe it is returning a copy. If we run the commands below, we can see that the output doesn't change if we change the original input object with an in-place operation.

```python
>>> import numpy as np
>>> a = np.asarray([np.nan, 1, 2, 3, np.nan])
>>> b = np.isnan(a)
>>> b
array([True, False, False, False,  True])
>>> a.sort()
>>> a
array([ 1.,  2.,  3., nan, nan])
>>> b
array([True, False, False, False,  True])
```

That being said, it should be safe to remove the copy. If we get errors from removing the copy, then we can add this quirk as a comment.

body:
Be careful with mutable types in the arguments, they are evaluated at module-time. To fix this, you can set them to None and add the default in the function body

```python
def foo(a = None):
   a = [1,2,3] is a is None else a
```
author: FrancescoSaverioZuppichini

body:
Just a personal style note. You can avoid early returns. In the above snippet if data_format is wrong x will be None

```python
    def forward(self, x):
        if self.data_format == "channels_last":
            x = torch.nn.functional.layer_norm(x, self.normalized_shape, self.weight, self.bias, self.eps)
        elif self.data_format == "channels_first":
            u = x.mean(1, keepdim=True)
            s = (x - u).pow(2).mean(1, keepdim=True)
            x = (x - u) / torch.sqrt(s + self.eps)
            x = self.weight[:, None, None] * x + self.bias[:, None, None]
        return x
```

Instead of breaking it, could we maybe deprecate it? IMO, there are still quite some people that train models without using our `Trainer`, our `DataCollators`, or official examples. In such cases, I can see people passing labels to the tokenizer:

```
tokenizer(label_str, return_tensors="pt", padding=True)
```

=> in this case the labels would be padded with `0's` which if we remove this part won't be ignored anymore. So IMO it would be nice to go through a deprecation cycle here

body:
`token_type_ids` is still used in the realm(bert) model inside `RealmReader`. We just don't use `token_type_ids` as the candidate mask for convenience and use the newly added `block_mask` instead, because I realized the former approach would result in `[SEP]` token occurring in the predicted answer spans, which is explained [here](https://github.com/huggingface/transformers/pull/15297#issue-1111824123).

This is indeed a breaking change. However, since users would not directly leverage `RealmReader` (`RealmReader` 
is an inner model of `RealmForOpenQA`, and the `relevance_score` input of `RealmReader` needs to be firstly computed in `RealmForOpenQA`), IMO I think this would not affect existing codes.
author: qqaatw

body:
Okay. I tested if we firstly call model.to("cuda") and then call `model.block_embedding_to("cpu")`, the gpu memory occupied by `block_emb` could be released, and the training ran normally on single RTX 2080Ti.

Note that one potential scenario is that if one's gpu has only 6 GiB memory like RTX 2060, the model will not be able to run training/inference on GPU following the above workflow because `model.to("cuda")` will consume roughly 8~9 GiB memory if `block_emb` is also sent to GPU, and the cuda out of memory error will occur.

Instead, if we override `model.to(...)`, we can do the following workflows:

with 40 GiB gpu memory like A100:
```
# training / inference (I think this will do full forward pass on GPU? let me know if I'm wrong)
model.to("cuda")
model.block_embedding_to("cuda")
```

with 11 GiB gpu memory like RTX 2080Ti:
```
# training (memory will not be enough to store gradients if block_emb is also on GPU)
model.to("cuda")

# inference
model.to("cuda")
model.block_embedding_to("cuda")
```

with 6 GiB gpu memory like RTX 2060:

```
# inference (not sure if training is possible)
model.to("cuda") # roughly consume 4 GiB
```

We can probably add FAISS support in the future, which is more compatible with PyTorch than ScaNN.
author: qqaatw

body: Agree with you!

body:
Thanks a lot for your work on this. I've only left comments on the modeling and configuration file as I understand it's a work in progress. I tried to leave comments on the bits that didn't have TODO remove on them, or come directly from the original implementation.

In general:
- we don't use `assert` statements in modeling file, but tests that raise the appropriate error type.
- every docstring should be written in Markdown and follow our usual conventions. Docstrings are essential for the user-facing classes (model, model outputs) but not necessarily the building blocks where a comment in the code is more than enough. A docstring that says "forward pass" for the method `forward` is really not needed (either needs to be expanded but can most likely be removed)
- we don't usually using typing of each variable, and adding all the `Tensor` (everything is a tensor, surprise surprise), `bool` or `int` are not useful and only make the code harder to read IMO. You should focus on putting those for objects with a surprising type only. In most cases, picking good variable names will make the type obvious to the reader.
- one-letter variable names should be banned
- ever model class in the maskformer should be prefixed with `MaskFormer`. This allows researcher to easily mix and match blocks from different modeling files.
author: sgugger

body:
I don't fully understand why some conversations were resolved without applying the suggestion in them or applying the suggestion.

The changes to the Swin model would need to go in a separate PR, with explanations on why they are necessary (for instance I don't get why we need to output those dimensions and can't infer them from the hidden states).

The feature extractor should be added to the main init.

As discussed:
- we shouldn't introduce a dependency on `einops` just for this model
- variable names should be descriptive and not one-letter long
- the documentation should follow the standardized format
- there is no need for all the `Tensor` type annotations
- the model outputs should be limited to tensors pre-last activations and any kind of post-processing should be either done in an example of use or the feature extractor decode method.

Just a matter of easy typing for me :) not that important but something I keep in mind. cc @sgugger

body:
This works for me! Something to keep in mind here (might be a bit complex), is that git is aware of what commits have been pushed to the remote only when it has done pushing that commit.

It means the following:

```
repo.git_commit(...)
repo.git_push(blocking=False)
...
repo.git_commit(...)
repo.git_push(blocking=False) <--- if the previous push was not finished, it will push the two commits instead of the last one, resulting in a 2x bigger (and slower) push
```

This means that if the pushes are very frequent, or with very big models, then it wouldn't be surprising to have each `git_push` push the previous commit(s) alongside it, resulting in each following invocation pushing bigger and bigger pushes.

One way to prevent that would be to ensure that all previous pushes have been finished before starting a new one. I'm not sure there is a perfect solution to this.
author: LysandreJik

body:
Hi Omar! I just checked your colab, and ran it. You have to wait for a solid 15 minutes after the fact to see the weights on your repo as Colab is uploading at an excruciatingly slow speed (those pushes during training are asynchronous to avoid slowing down training).

`trainer.push_in_progress` give you the job that is pushing, you can check its `stdout` attribute to see the progress it makes, its `is_done` attribute to see if it's finished or not and its `stderr` attribute to check if there was an error or not.

You can see on [this repo](https://huggingface.co/sgugger/debug-example2) that I eventually got my weights pushed with your code :-)

body:
This was indeed my first solution too, but I thought it was safer to propose a solution that would keep the same ids that were hardcoded before in the code.

Using the default values:
```python
bos_token="<s>"
eos_token="</s>"
sep_token="</s>"
cls_token="<s>"
unk_token="<unk>"
pad_token="<pad>"
mask_token="<mask>"

```
Here is what it would look like with your solution:
```python
dict_sylvain = {
    str(token): token_id
    for token_id, token in enumerate([bos_token, pad_token, eos_token, unk_token, sep_token, cls_token])
}
print(dict_sylvain)
# {'<s>': 5, '<pad>': 1, '</s>': 4, '<unk>': 3}
```
With mine:
```python
dict_lucile = {
    token: token_id
    for token_id, token in enumerate(
        dict.fromkeys([str(bos_token), str(pad_token), str(eos_token), str(unk_token), str(sep_token), str(cls_token)]).keys()
    )
}
print(dict_lucile)
# {'<s>': 0, '<pad>': 1, '</s>': 2, '<unk>': 3} <- this is what was hardcoded before
```

~~Note that I've changed a little bit the format of this part of the code in [this commit](https://github.com/huggingface/transformers/pull/15328/commits/37a40df37903a932c2f951cbd33acb684246bae7) in order to only use once `str(Xxx)`~~ Actually I remember why I did this: if we ever have `"<s>"` and `AddedToken("<s>")`, it must appear in the end only once in the ids count.
author: SaulLu
SaulLu

body:
I was a little too enthusiastic, but in fact it seems to me that one cannot do without putting the casting of the string on each `Xxx_token` because we must check here that the token is not part of the list of strings of the special tokens - which can be of type `AddedToken` or `str` - . But the good new is that the tokenizer already have an attribute with this list `self.all_special_tokens`.

I'm changing it in a [new commit.](https://github.com/huggingface/transformers/pull/15328/commits/c986e6a844d53225db760eed0c75a91dec2c6ba2) Sorry again for the confusion
author: SaulLu

body:
I have already done tensor parallelization of 70 models when I am developing parallelformers, I've only found two models that use this method in the transformers now. However, when we parallelizing tensor dimension, this information could also be very useful, so it's a good idea to keep it here.

In the case of a qkv or kv fused layer, it must be deconstructed through separate processing and then parallelized for parallelization. this mapping is necessary to support models like GPT2, TransfoXL. or do you have any other ideas to automate detecting this? @RezaYazdaniAminabadi @lucasleesw Note thet we also need to consider about cross-attention (kv fusion) via `GPT2Model` + `EncoderDecoderModel` framework in the Transformers. there are cases where it does not have triple the size.
author: hyunwoongko

body:
I have already done tensor parallelization of 70 models when I am developing parallelformers, I've only found two models that use this method in the transformers now. However, when we parallelizing tensor dimension, this information could also be very useful, so it's a good idea to keep it here.

@RezaYazdaniAminabadi In the case of DS-inference engine, it is no longer necessary to input a variable called conv_linear_layer from the user or manage it inside the engine. Currently, ds-inference-general checks whether it is reversed by distinguishing whether it is a Conv1D layer or a nn.Linear layer, but in the case of TransfoXL, there is a reversed nn.Linear layer (`in_features` and `out_features` are exchanged). So, to support models like TransfoXL, this way is better. (It's more general.)

Let me add a mapping for t5:
```
'T5PreTrinedModel': {
        'column_parallel':['Attention.q', 'Attention.k', 'Attention.v', 'DenseReluDense.wi', 'relative_attention_bias'],
        'row_parallel':['Attention.o', 'DenseReluDense.wo'],
        'update_attrs':['n_heads', 'inner_dim']
```

body:
@sgugger Can you let me know which version this was applied from? The users of the lower transformers version still need this in the OSLO.

To add a little more, the transformers will also have this mapping, but the OSLO will have it internally as well. This is because this mapping class does not exist for users of lower versions of the transformers. Users with lower versions will use the mapping inside OSLO and users with higher versions will use the mapping from transformers. So this check is not required in transformers, but still required in OSLO.

author: Narsil

body:
Quick note on test readability.

Tests only asset is that they are readable, meaning you should know just by looking at them if they are correct or not.
That's why IMHO they should contain as little logic as possible (no `if` no `for`).

The iteration over ALL tokenizer classes is necessary to ensure some things are consistent across HF ecosystem.

But, my  point is that 

```python
 kwargs.update({"truncation_side": truncation_side})
```
is actually modifiying state much earlier that where the test actually lies, also 

```python
self.assertEqual(tokenizer_r.truncation_side, truncation_side)
```
Actually tests against a variable and not a value, meaning you can do a later update to the code, that actually is BREAKING and you're not going to see it, because the variable gets updated too by the code and the tests, while being unchanged has actually changed values, hiding it entirely the misbehavior from the test.


```suggestion
                if self.test_rust_tokenizer:
                    tokenizer_r = self.rust_tokenizer_class.from_pretrained(pretrained_name, truncation_side="left", **kwargs)
                    self.assertEqual(tokenizer_r.truncation_side, "left")
```

Is IMHO, a much better test. No state, no variable used as the reference value.

All the `ifs` everywhere are also littering readability IMO. Not sure what's the best course of action is there (what's in `self.tokenizers_list`, doesn't it also contains information about the supported classes ?)

author: SaulLu

body:
> Actually tests against a variable and not a value, meaning you can do a later update to the code, that actually is BREAKING and you're not going to see it, because the variable gets updated too by the code and the tests, will being unchanged has actually changed values, hiding it entirely the misbehavior from the test.

This is indeed a good point. I modified the test according to the template you propose. I had in mind that the arguments could possibly be already present in kwargs, but if it is the case we will override the test for the tokenizer in question.

> Tests only asset is that they are readable, meaning you should know just by looking at them if they are correct or not.
That's why IMHO they should contain as little logic as possible (no if no for).

> All the ifs everywhere are also littering readability IMO. Not sure what's the best course of action is there (what's in self.tokenizers_list, doesn't it also contains information about the supported classes ?)

Looking at your comments on readability, I understand them - I guess it's also a matter of preference. I've already reorganized the test to reduce the number of ifs to 2. Is that better? Unforthunatly, `self.tokenizers_list` only contain the fast class that is why we need to use `if self.test_rust_tokenizer` and `test_slow_tokenizer` - as a side note, what you raised can be a good idea for refactoring the tests and improve their global readability. Regarding the `for` loop, I used it because it's the same template used in a lot of other tests of this class, So, I think that for this PR to keep the same format is adapted.

The other alternative I see for this test would consist in splitting it in 2:
```python
    def test_truncation_side_in_kwargs_slow(self):
        if not self.test_slow_tokenizer:
            continue

        for tokenizer, pretrained_name, kwargs in self.tokenizers_list:
            with self.subTest(f"{tokenizer.__class__.__name__} ({pretrained_name})"):
                tokenizer_p = self.tokenizer_class.from_pretrained(
                    pretrained_name, truncation_side="left", **kwargs
                )
                self.assertEqual(tokenizer_p.truncation_side, "left")

                tokenizer_p = self.tokenizer_class.from_pretrained(
                    pretrained_name, truncation_side="right", **kwargs
                )
                self.assertEqual(tokenizer_p.truncation_side, "right")

                self.assertRaises(
                    ValueError,
                    self.tokenizer_class.from_pretrained,
                    pretrained_name,
                    truncation_side="unauthorized",
                    **kwargs,
                )

    def test_truncation_side_in_kwargs_fast(self):
        if not self.test_rust_tokenizer:
            continue

        for tokenizer, pretrained_name, kwargs in self.tokenizers_list:
            with self.subTest(f"{tokenizer.__class__.__name__} ({pretrained_name})"):
                tokenizer_r = self.rust_tokenizer_class.from_pretrained(
                    pretrained_name, truncation_side="left", **kwargs
                )
                self.assertEqual(tokenizer_r.truncation_side, "left")

                tokenizer_r = self.rust_tokenizer_class.from_pretrained(
                    pretrained_name, truncation_side="right", **kwargs
                )
                self.assertEqual(tokenizer_r.truncation_side, "right")

                self.assertRaises(
                    ValueError,
                    self.rust_tokenizer_class.from_pretrained,
                    pretrained_name,
                    truncation_side="unauthorized",
                    **kwargs,
                )
```

body:
Looks good to me (wondering why the PR is called "standardize instance segmentation models outputs"?).

* `xxxForPixelClassification` => both semantic and panoptic segmentation are pixel classification tasks, right? Although in semantic segmentation, one only predicts a single label per pixel, whereas one predicts two labels per pixel in case of panoptic segmentation. If we're going with `xxxForPixelClassification` and `xxxForPanopticSegmentation`, people would be confused and that's not really consistent. So I would stick to `xxxForSemanticSegmentation`.

There's also depth estimation, which is per-pixel regression (if I understand correctly, I'm not entirely familiar with it). Would we then have `xxxForPixelRegression`? However, currently we also tackle both regression and classification with `xxxForSequenceClassification` models.

author: FrancescoSaverioZuppichini

body:
I have a doubt about the naming. `logits` is usually used to indicate un-normalized vectors used to compute a downstream task (e.g. classification after a softmax layer). If that is the case, return this output class is unfeasible for some models, e.g. `MaskFormer`. 

In `MaskFormer` we compute the final segmentation masks using two vectors

```python
def forward(self, *args, **kwargs):
        outputs: MaskFormerOutput = self.model(*args, **kwargs)
        # mask classes has shape [BATCH, QUERIES, CLASSES + 1]
        # remove the null class `[..., :-1]`
        masks_classes: Tensor = outputs.preds_logits.softmax(dim=-1)[..., :-1]
        # mask probs has shape [BATCH, QUERIES, HEIGHT, WIDTH]
        masks_probs: Tensor = outputs.preds_masks.sigmoid()
        # now we want to sum over the queries,
        # $ out_{c,h,w} =  \sum_q p_{q,c} * m_{q,h,w} $
        # where $ softmax(p) \in R^{q, c} $ is the mask classes
        # and $ sigmoid(m) \in R^{q, h, w}$ is the mask probabilities
        # b(atch)q(uery)c(lasses), b(atch)q(uery)h(eight)w(idth)
        segmentation: Tensor = torch.einsum("bqc, bqhw -> bchw", masks_classes, masks_probs)

        return MaskFormerForSemanticSegmentationOutput(segmentation=segmentation, **outputs)
```        
Returning a vector `logits` of shape `batch_size, config.num_labels, height, width)` is impossible due. I see two possible solutions

@NielsRogge opened an [issue](https://github.com/facebookresearch/MaskFormer/issues/60) to ask MaskFormer author if is possible to return `logits` from their model
author: FrancescoSaverioZuppichini

body: We might have to treat `MaskFormer` with an exception (in the sense we will need the raw output to have both logits and prediction masks to be able to compute the segmentation) the same way we handle the model for QA with beam search in XLNert. That class would then not be in the `AutoModelForSemanticSegmentation` as it doesn't have the same API, but we would need one version of `MaskFormerForSemanticSegmentation` with the same API (it might perform as well as this fancy one with some fine-tuning).
author: sgugger

body:
I don't have sufficient experience with computer vision and these specific tasks to review the content of the PR, so my review is on the:

- coherence of this auto model with the rest of the auto models 
- backwards compatibility

I think this PR is great as it's a good opportunity to observe if our "experimental" approach works or not. I think it shows that it lacks quite a few aspects that would make it robust:

- We do not systematically mention that models are experimental on their modeling page, which is an issue. It should be very visible from the users.
- This is an urgent refactor, but ideally this should go through a correct deprecation cycle. I think we need to be clear that this is an exceptional instance where we do not respect backwards compatibility for experimental reasons.
- In order to patch this, I think we'll need to:
    - Tweet (can be from personal accounts) about the update
    - Create a forum post
    - Create a pinned github issue

Thanks for working on this, @sgugger!
author: LysandreJik

body:
Regarding `legacy_logits`, I wonder if it wouldn't be better, API-wise, to have the "old" logits returned according to a flag `legacy_output` or something similar. I understand the need for the breaking change, but I think it would be simpler on users if they didn't have to update their model outputs, but update their model instantiation. 

I think it's easier as it doesn't require diving into their previously working code to see what needs to be changed, they would just need to update the config init/model init.

The model would then return a `SequenceClassifierOutput` with the previous values when instantiated with `legacy_output`, and would return the new, better `SemanticSegmentationModelOutput` when nothing is set.

I would also output a [`FutureWarning`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/exceptions.html#FutureWarning) when users either instantiate the model, or during their first forward pass. I think the latter is best, as if they have not updated their code and it crashes, the warning will be just above their crash.
author: LysandreJik

body:
To run the checkpoint conversion script:
```
python -m transformers.models.data2vec.convert_data2vec_audio_original_pytorch_checkpoint_to_pytorch --checkpoint_path <your_path_to>/audio_base_ls.pt --pytorch_dump_folder_path <your_output_path> --not_finetuned
```
`audio_base_ls.pt` can be downloaded from [here](https://dl.fbaipublicfiles.com/fairseq/data2vec/audio_base_ls.pt).  
You will see two print statements showing that the forward pass matches up until the addition of convolutional positional embeddings.  
This script was modified from the [Wav2Vec2's checkpoint conversion script](https://github.com/huggingface/transformers/blob/master/src/transformers/models/wav2vec2/convert_wav2vec2_original_pytorch_checkpoint_to_pytorch.py)

body:
BTW, I'm not a huge fan in general that we make the Transformers library more and more dependent on the HF Hub. I don't think design decisions of a fully open-sourced library like Transformers should be driven in any way by higher level tools that make use of the library, like the HF Hub. 

Think another small example of this is that the widgets on the Hub are tied to the naming of classes in Transformers.

So far these are very minor dependencies and can easily be respected from the Transformers side, but they do limit Transformer's design a bit unnecessarily IMO and slow down open-source contributions (*e.g.* how would an open-source contributor know the points mentioned above?)

Keen to hear your opinion on this, also cc @LysandreJik

body:
What we do here is equivalent to what is done below for `# linear layer` - I don't understand why we've added this. Was some code failing before? 

`pt_tensor.transpose(2, 1, 0)` is the same as `pt_tensor.T` and if `pt_tuple_key[-1] == "weight" and pt_tensor.ndim == 3 and not is_key_or_prefix_key_in_dict(pt_tuple_key)` is true then `pt_tuple_key[-1] == "weight" and not is_key_or_prefix_key_in_dict(pt_tuple_key)` is also True.

We should avoid at all cost to add more complexity to those weight conversion statements and keep things as simple as possible. Remember that all PT<->Flax conversions depend on this code and we should not clutter be extra careful here. If we add a new statement like this, there has to be at least a test that ensures that this change is needed. At the moment I cannot think of a single use case where this is the case -> we already had 1D-conv layer conversions working for Flax Wav2Vec2 <-> PT Wav2Vec2 

=> IMO we should revert this PR. The comments could indeed be improved however, so I'm happy to change the comment `# conv layer` to `# 2D - conv layer` and `# linear layer` to `# linear and 1D - conv layer`

author: FremyCompany

body:
Ok, then I am unsure we should add that warning.

`pipeline` is the function where we can hide **a lot** of magic.
but the actual classes like `AutomaticSpeechRecognitionPipeline` should be very "standard" pythonic without any magic.

For instance, you cannot pass it a `str` like `"facebook/wav2vec2-base-960h"` you need to pass the real model. It also does not attempt to prevent you from passing your own very custom object enabling advanced usage.

So how do we enable users using a `model with lm` without the `decoder` and without a warning ? We cannot with your proposal (unless adding a magic flag to suppress the warning, which is clunky).

So currently I would say we should keep the current behavior:
- `pipeline` works out magic, makes the best educated guess we can possibly can with all the magic. Gives out warnings when it cannot create optional things (like `decoder`).
- `AutomaticSpeechRecognitionPipeline` is for users that know a little what they do, we make no attempt  of blocking you. But also you can make mistakes too..

```suggestion
```
One should more or less never use `np.random.random()` in a Flax model because it makes the model non-deterministic. Instead it's better to force the model to pass a PRNGKey at `.apply(...)` (shown here: https://github.com/huggingface/transformers/blob/8ce133063120683018b214fe10d1449e4c2401da/src/transformers/models/wav2vec2/modeling_flax_wav2vec2.py#L1197) and then retrieve it with `self.make_rng(...)` as shown here: https://github.com/huggingface/transformers/blob/8ce133063120683018b214fe10d1449e4c2401da/src/transformers/models/wav2vec2/modeling_flax_wav2vec2.py#L743 . Think `layerdrop_prob` is really neat but so far we have just left it out in Flax. So maybe we can just remove it here for now as well and only add it in the "correct" way in a follow-up PR (not super important though)

author: mingboiz

body:
Thank you so much for all the work you've done, I've left a comment to possibly improve the tests, it's obviously very debatable, look forward to reading your opinions on it!

While testing the export of the fast version of DebertaV2 and DebertaV3, I realize that there are unfortunately additional difficulties:
 ```python
from transformers import DebertaV2TokenizerFast, DebertaV2Tokenizer

model_name_v2 = "microsoft/deberta-v2-xlarge"
model_name_v3 = "microsoft/deberta-v3-large"

tokenizer_v2_f = DebertaV2TokenizerFast.from_pretrained(model_name_v2)
tokenizer_v2_s = DebertaV2Tokenizer.from_pretrained(model_name_v2)

tokenizer_v3_f = DebertaV2TokenizerFast.from_pretrained(model_name_v3)
tokenizer_v3_s = DebertaV2Tokenizer.from_pretrained(model_name_v3)
```
 ```python
# Test 1    
text = u"\u0041\u0302\u0300" + " " + u"\u0041\u0302\u0300" # Ầ  Ầ 
print(f"{'tokenizer_v2_f:':20} ", tokenizer_v2_f.convert_ids_to_tokens(tokenizer_v2_f.encode(text)))
print(f"{'tokenizer_v2_s:':20} ", tokenizer_v2_s.convert_ids_to_tokens(tokenizer_v2_s.encode(text)))
# tokenizer_v2_f:       ['[CLS]', '▁Â', '▁Â', '[SEP]']
# tokenizer_v2_s:       ['[CLS]', '▁', '[UNK]', '▁', '[UNK]', '[SEP]']

print(f"{'tokenizer_v2_f:':20} ", [token.encode('raw_unicode_escape') for token in tokenizer_v2_f.convert_ids_to_tokens(tokenizer_v2_f.encode(text, add_special_tokens=False))])
# tokenizer_v3_f:       [b'\\u2581\xc2', b'\\u2581\xc2']
```
It seems that the tokenizer fast does a normalization of the text that is not done in the tokenizer slow. I haven't had time to dig deeper to find out if this is a "rare" case or not. I think it would be nice to understand where this difference comes from.

```python
# Test 2
text = u"\u1ea6" + " " + u"\u1ea6" # Ầ Ầ
print(f"{'tokenizer_v3_f:':20} ", tokenizer_v3_f.convert_ids_to_tokens(tokenizer_v3_f.encode(text)))
print(f"{'tokenizer_v3_s:':20} ", tokenizer_v3_s.convert_ids_to_tokens(tokenizer_v3_s.encode(text)))
# tokenizer_v3_f:       ['[CLS]', '▁', '[UNK]', '▁', '[UNK]', '[SEP]']
# tokenizer_v3_s:       ['[CLS]', '▁', '<0xE1>', '<0xBA>', '<0xA6>', '▁', '<0xE1>', '<0xBA>', '<0xA6>', '[SEP]']
 ```
This case shows that the debertaV3 sentence piece tokenizer uses the byte fallback feature. This poses 2 difficulties: 1) the byte fallback feature is not implemented in `tokenizers` at the moment (it's in the roadmap) and 2) our current reading of the sentencepiece proto model does not allow us to know if this feature is used by the sentencepiece model (I'll open another issue on this subject as it's not directly related to this PR).
author: SaulLu

body:
Some small suggestions for writing new tests, don't hesitate to share your thoughts if you don't agree! :blush:

- As we know that `DebertaV2` has a fast version I think we can remove the `if self.test_rust_tokenizer`
- As we check that tokenization is equal to the same token list twice I think we can assign the objective token list to the same variable - this avoids that a typo / difference between the 2 lists can slip in without us noticing, especially in the futur.
- I think that the last `self.assertListEqual(tokens, rust_tokens)` test is not useful as we have already checked that before the 2 lists were equal to the same list
- In general, I prefer to test `tokenizer.convert_ids_to_tokens(tokenizer.encode())` because depending on how the `tokenize` method it kind hide actual problems

I take the opportunity to show what this would give on the first test `test_do_lower_case`, but if you're agree I think that we should apply the changes to all the tests :smile: 
```suggestion
    def test_do_lower_case(self):
        sequence = " \tHeLLo!how  \n Are yoU?  "
        tokens_target = ["▁hello", "!", "how", "▁are", "▁you", "?"]
        
        tokenizer = DebertaV2Tokenizer(SAMPLE_VOCAB, do_lower_case=True)
        tokens = tokenizer.convert_ids_to_tokens(tokenizer.encode(sequence))

        self.assertListEqual(tokens, tokens_target)

        rust_tokenizer = DebertaV2TokenizerFast(SAMPLE_VOCAB, do_lower_case=True)
        rust_tokens = rust_tokenizer.convert_ids_to_tokens(rust_tokenizer.encode(sequence))

        self.assertListEqual(rust_tokens, tokens_target)
```
author: SaulLu

body:
@SaulLu thank you for your comments! yeah I agree with you, but I wanted to keep as close as possible to the format of existing tests. So if it's fine I shall modify the rest of the tests accordingly! Just a doubt with regards to `test_full_tokenizer` test - should we keep it as it is? We are only comparing the each lists once, and it seems to me introduce more verbosity if I was to assign it like this:

```
    def test_full_tokenizer(self):
        tokenizer = DebertaV2Tokenizer(SAMPLE_VOCAB, keep_accents=True)

        tokens = tokenizer.tokenize("This is a test")
        tokens_target = ["▁", "T", "his", "▁is", "▁a", "▁test"]
        self.assertListEqual(tokens, tokens_target)

        ids_target = [13, 1, 4398, 25, 21, 1289]
        self.assertListEqual(tokenizer.convert_tokens_to_ids(tokens), ids_target)

        tokens = tokenizer.tokenize("I was born in 92000, and this is falsé.")
        tokens_target = ["▁", "I", "▁was", "▁born", "▁in", "▁9", "2000", ",", "▁and", "▁this", "▁is", "▁fal", "s", "é", "."]
        self.assertListEqual(tokens, tokens_target)

        ids = tokenizer.convert_tokens_to_ids(tokens)
        ids_target = [13, 1, 23, 386, 19, 561, 3050, 15, 17, 48, 25, 8256, 18, 1, 9]
        self.assertListEqual(ids, ids_target)

        back_tokens = tokenizer.convert_ids_to_tokens(ids)
        back_tokens_target = ["▁", "<unk>", "▁was", "▁born", "▁in", "▁9", "2000", ",", "▁and", "▁this", "▁is", "▁fal", "s", "<unk>", "."]
        self.assertListEqual(back_tokens, back_tokens_target)
```

Shall I just leave this test as it is, and modify the rest accordingly? thanks 😄
author: mingboiz

body:
@SaulLu Sorry for the late response, I looked into your first point - can I check if I'm going on the right path here? It appears that the difference is due to for Debertav2 - the slow tokenizer uses the backend Sentencepiece tokenizer to tokenize https://github.com/huggingface/transformers/blob/f7196f2e63b14e9fbb4ad664e71912aab3b484cf/src/transformers/models/deberta_v2/tokenization_deberta_v2.py#L403-L410

whereas the fast tokenizer seem to use the TokenizerFast from the tokenizers library converted using the provided vocab files from the original SPM, so the mismatch in normalization is due to the backend TokenizerFast of tokenizers?

```
>>> from transformers import DebertaV2TokenizerFast, DebertaV2Tokenizer
>>> model_name_v2 = "microsoft/deberta-v2-xlarge"
>>> model_name_v3 = "microsoft/deberta-v3-large"
>>> tokenizer_v2_s = DebertaV2Tokenizer.from_pretrained(model_name_v2)
>>> tokenizer_v3_f = DebertaV2TokenizerFast.from_pretrained(model_name_v3)
>>> backend_tokenizer = tokenizer_v2_s._tokenizer
>>> text = u"\u0041\u0302\u0300" + " " + u"\u0041\u0302\u0300"
>>> tokenizer_v2_s.convert_ids_to_tokens(backend_tokenizer.spm.encode(text))
['▁', '[UNK]', '▁', '[UNK]']
>>> tokenizer_v2_f.convert_ids_to_tokens(tokenizer_v2_f.encode(text))
['[CLS]', '▁Â', '▁Â', '[SEP]']
```
I've also tested using some of the other tests - [French](https://github.com/huggingface/transformers/blob/8e0d3b427f4936cd0318996d4c3dbdd779d16641/tests/camembert/test_tokenization_camembert.py#L119-L125) and [Japanese](https://github.com/huggingface/transformers/blob/8e0d3b427f4936cd0318996d4c3dbdd779d16641/tests/bert_japanese/test_tokenization_bert_japanese.py#L224)

```
>>> text = "À l'instar des réseaux de neurones récurrents (RNN), les transformeurs sont conçus "
>>> tokenizer_v2_s.convert_ids_to_tokens(tokenizer_v2_s.encode(text))
['[CLS]', '▁À', '▁l', "'", 'in', 'star', '▁des', '▁ré', 's', 'eaux', '▁de', '▁neuron', 'es', '▁ré', 'current', 's', '▁(', 'R', 'NN', '),', '▁les', '▁transform', 'eurs', '▁sont', '▁con', 'çu', 's', '[SEP]']
>>> tokenizer_v2_f.convert_ids_to_tokens(tokenizer_v2_f.encode(text))
['[CLS]', '▁À', '▁l', "'", 'in', 'star', '▁des', '▁ré', 's', 'eaux', '▁de', '▁neuron', 'es', '▁ré', 'current', 's', '▁(', 'R', 'NN', '),', '▁les', '▁transform', 'eurs', '▁sont', '▁con', 'çu', 's', '[SEP]']
>>> text = "こんにちは、世界。 \nこんばんは、世界。"
>>> tokenizer_v2_f.convert_ids_to_tokens(tokenizer_v2_f.encode(text))
['[CLS]', '▁', 'こ', 'ん', 'に', 'ち', 'は', '、', '世', '界', '。', '▁', 'こ', 'ん', 'ば', 'ん', 'は', '、', '世', '界', '。', '[SEP]']
>>> tokenizer_v2_s.convert_ids_to_tokens(tokenizer_v2_s.encode(text))
['[CLS]', '▁', 'こ', 'ん', 'に', 'ち', 'は', '、', '世', '界', '。', '▁', 'こ', 'ん', 'ば', 'ん', 'は', '、', '世', '界', '。', '[SEP]']
```
Strangely the french test with the À doesn't appear to be affected here though
author: mingboiz

body:
Hi @mingboiz ,

> @SaulLu thank you for your comments! yeah I agree with you, but I wanted to keep as close as possible to the format of existing tests. So if it's fine I shall modify the rest of the tests accordingly! Just a doubt with regards to test_full_tokenizer test - should we keep it as it is? We are only comparing the each lists once, and it seems to me introduce more verbosity if I was to assign it like this:

Indeed for the `test_full_tokenizer` test, I don't really have a preference for one or the other. I trust you :smile: 

### Questions about Test 1
> @SaulLu Sorry for the late response, I looked into your first point - can I check if I'm going on the right path here? It appears that the difference is due to for Debertav2 - the slow tokenizer uses the backend Sentencepiece tokenizer to tokenized 

No worries! Thank you very much for spending this amount of time for this PR! I think you are on the right track. I also spent some time investigating the problem and I agree with you: I think the difference comes from a small known difference between the normalization step in sentencepiece and the way this normalization is treated in tokenizers with the `tokenizers.normalizers.Precompiled` component (see comment [here](https://github.com/huggingface/transformers/issues/15970#issuecomment-1081639998) for more details). 

Indeed, the normalization used by `"microsoft/deberta-v2-xlarge"` is `nmt_nfkc`. The sentencepiece library stores [here](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/google/sentencepiece/master/data/nmt_nfkc.tsv)  the replacements to be carried out for this normalization. In particular, we can see the line `41 302 300	1EA6	# Ầ => Ầ` which means that `u"\u0041\u0302\u0300"` will be replaced by `u"\u1ea6"`.  And we can verify that `u"\u1ea6"` is indeed encode as an `unknown` token by our tokenizer.

**TL;DR:** I think that the origin of the difference is identified and since it is a known and quite rare "bug" I think that we can say that the **investigation for test 1 is resolved.** Is that OK?

### Questions about Test 2

I haven't put a detailed message here, but I recently [merged a PR](https://github.com/huggingface/transformers/pull/16629) to at least get a warning when a user tries to convert an spm tokenizer that uses the bytefallback feature to a fast tokenizer. I think this warning would be sufficient for now if the tokenizer fast file is not pushed to one of the `"microsoft/deberta-v3-Xxx` repos.

_I'm just pinging one of the transformers maintainers about this to get a second opinion._

@LysandreJik , @sgugger , @patrickvonplaten , @patil-suraj (I don't want to take too much time, you can just read the following paragraph) for context this PR aims to add a fast version of the tokenizer for `DebertaV2`. This class can currently be used to load `"microsoft/deberta-v2-Xxx` and `"microsoft/deberta-v3-Xxx` uploaded to the hub. One of the differences between the fast and slow versions is that with our fast tokenizers we can't enable the bytefallback option (it's in the tokenizers lib roadmap but not done). This feature is used by `"microsoft/deberta-v3-Xxx`. The current PR will only push a warning to the user that this feature is missing (cf PR #16629). Are you ok with this solution? 
I want to draw your attention to the fact that if we want to add later in our [`SpmConverter` class](https://github.com/huggingface/transformers/blob/d8269eb4d55e4e43c9b885807a82cc428783a573/src/transformers/convert_slow_tokenizer.py#L420) - which is used by several tokenizers - we will have to be very careful to push for smooth breaking change as `"microsoft/deberta-v3-Xxx` is the first tokenizer - that I'm aware of - that is in this case.
author: SaulLu

body: I'm good with this solution, as long as no fast tokenizer file are updated yet for checkpoints using this option (so we can add proper ones without breaking changes once the option is supported in tokenizers).

body:
This relates more generally to a problem of consistency in the `tokenize` method between the different types of tokenizer (cf issue #15138). I think it is the good place for me to say that personally, I think the tokenize method should always return something equivalent to `tokenizer.convert_ids_to_tokens(tokenizer.encode(text))`, but this is not the case for all unigram-based tokenizers (it's like this inside the `tokenizers` lib and a lot of tokenizers are in this situation). Here this change will make `ŧokenize` not equivalent to `tokenizer.convert_ids_to_tokens(tokenizer.encode(text))`.

If you think that making this change is a mistake, please share! :smile:

body: Maybe the comments aren't super clear here, but the logic is the following. For both inputs (batch_1 and batch_2) we want to know which word ids we are allowed to generate for the next step. The logits vector is of dim 5 thus covering the word ids [0-4] . Now the word ids [1], [4] are always forbidden, which is why we have `True` for both output lists in the last place [4th logit] and the second one [1st]. The other three sequences are then responsible for setting the other values (0th and 3rd for the first batch) and (0th and 2nd for the second batch)
author: patrickvonplaten

author: patrickvonplaten

body: Gotcha -- e.g. because both batches end with a `1` and `[1, 0]` is a bad sequence, generating a `0` in the next step is forbidden. Thanks 👍
author: gante

body:
I'm unsure though! Let's say we have two `LogitsProcessor`s in our list. `processor_1` takes `input_ids`, `scores` and `extra_data1` and `processor_2` takes `input_ids`, `scores` and `extra_data2`. Then the `__call__` to the `TFLogitsProcessorList` will need to have both `extra_data1` and `extra_data2`, but it will try to pass *both* to each `LogitsProcessor` and that will cause them to crash.

It seems like all `LogitsProcessor` classes with more than two arguments also need a `**kwargs` argument in their `__call__` method to catch extra unused arguments, or else they need to all have the same `__call__` signature.

body:
Thanks for adding this new example!

This PR tries to do too much at the same time however:
- there is breaking change in BeIT, so this model should be excluded from this PR and added separately once this one is merged, so we can take more action to mitigate the breaking change and be more backward compatible (in particular, the default value for the `use_mask_token` in the embedding should be the one given by the config)
- there is another breaking change in the initialization of ViT which should also go in a separate PR

Pay attention to the file you include in your PR, there are three here that are not necessary:
- a `.DS_Store` file
- models/swin/inference.py
- models/vit/inference.py
You should consider using a GUI for git (like GitKraken) if those were added by mistake.

body:
Excellent work, @eldarkurtic - thank you for working on this.

I visually checked that nothing was left out - I wonder whether we have all these activation functions tested - have you by chance grepped if all entries in the ACT2FN table appear at least once in the tests?

(note for future PRs: in general changing the order of the original code chunks and doing changes to it at the same time makes it much more difficult to review, but no need to change anything as I have already verified it - just had to scroll up and down a lot)

------------

Answering 2 of your questions:

> Are there any known barriers for usage of inplace versions of these activation functions? If you are not aware of any, I can push an update for this, so we could squeeze in some additional performance gains.

If I understand things correctly, inplace operations are in general frowned upon due to the subtle bugs they could lead to, but perhaps in a tiny scope it should be clear enough to avoid such bugs.

But main inplace ops become a problem when using various compilers and fusers.

I think we can provide an alternative function/class that implements the faster operations if users want to use those and then perhaps the user could choose which one to use via model config.

But try to add `@torch.jit.script` before the `forward` and see if the inplace operation is any faster than the jit'ed optimized version. I think jit should be faster or on par. When you benchmark remember to warm up the jit'ed functions - the first forward will be slow as it's compiling it first and then running it. So start benchmarking from the 2nd forward on. 

Let us know what you find - perhaps we can discuss these in another issue so we don't interfere with this PR.

> Are quick_gelu and gelu_fast implementations necessary now that the pytorch has a built-in implementation (nn.functional.gelu) which should be sufficiently fast?

HF transformers currently tries to support pytorch >= 1.4, which requires that we keep those in place.

If there is a faster version in the pytorch core, we should definitely swap it in when it's available.

But again, let's do the conversion to classes without changing anything else, and then do further PRs to modify things if need be.
author: stas00

body:
Thanks a lot for working on this! I agree with all Stas comments, and add a little bit more on the inplace: as this can lead to subtle bugs and activation functions are used in pretty much every model, I seriously doubt we can make the switch for fear of breaking changes.

We can however have an opt-in with an environment variable like `USE_INPLACE_ACTIVATIONS` to let the user switch to them if they want to.

author: stas00

body:
Overall a great test, @manuelciosici!

Do you have support for why checking that bnb gpu peak memory is higher tests that bnb works?

I extended the code to dump the allocated delta as well, 

```
            gpu_peak_memory = logs[0]["train_mem_gpu_peaked_delta"]
            gpu_alloc_memory = logs[0]["train_mem_gpu_alloc_delta"]
            loss = logs[0]["train_loss"]
            return gpu_peak_memory, gpu_alloc_memory, loss

        original_gpu_peak_memory, original_gpu_alloc_memory, original_loss = train_and_return_metrics(OptimizerNames.ADAMW_TORCH.value)
        bnb_gpu_peak_memory, bnb_gpu_alloc_memory, bnb_loss = train_and_return_metrics(OptimizerNames.ADAMW_BNB.value)

        print(f"{original_gpu_alloc_memory=:012d} {original_gpu_peak_memory=:012d} {original_gpu_alloc_memory+original_gpu_peak_memory=:012d}")
        print(f"     {bnb_gpu_alloc_memory=:012d}      {bnb_gpu_peak_memory=:012d}           {bnb_gpu_alloc_memory+bnb_gpu_peak_memory=:012d}")
```

and I get:

```
original_gpu_alloc_memory=000645350400 original_gpu_peak_memory=000243983360 original_gpu_alloc_memory+original_gpu_peak_memory=000889333760
     bnb_gpu_alloc_memory=000507030528      bnb_gpu_peak_memory=000382303232           bnb_gpu_alloc_memory+bnb_gpu_peak_memory=000889333760
```

Looking at that total of 2 deltas which are identical tells me that both optimizers required the exact same amount of gpu memory, so it didn't save anything. or in other words a card with less memory wouldn't have been enough to run this training.

(you need py3.8 to have the `{foo=}` trick to work)

author: stas00

body:
Here is how I understand the inner working of the BNB optimizer. Let's look at a single train iteration and how much memory optimizer states consume per param.

1. `forward`: 2 bytes
2. `backward`: 2 bytes
3. optimizer `step`: 2 + 6 bytes (due to dequantized to 8 bytes)

Assuming that `forward` activation memory has been released when `backward` has finished there should be some "spare" peak memory available to expand 2 to 8 bytes per param to perform the step. And there are some temps as well. I think in general it should be less than the additional 6 bytes per param. So the optimizer step is very likely to consume additional "peak" memory. But overall it should consume less peak memory since the 6 bytes aren't "held hostage" during all other stages besides the optimizer `step`.

Or is it possible that `backward` never released the `forward` activation memory in which case it makes total sense that the additional 6 bytes to dequantize 2 bytes will come 100% from the peak memory (which the card may not have).

The test you wrote shows that we end up with the exact same amount of total gpu memory used by the card to perform a training step including peak memory. Which either defeats the purpose of BNB or something is wrong here.

In other words, what we want to see is that alloc+peak gpu memory of BNB should be some 25%+ smaller than the same usage with normal AdamW. In other words one can use a smaller GPU card with BNB. This is because we expect:

(optim + weights + grads = total bytes per param)
- AdamW: 8 + 4 + 4 = 16
- BNB:       2  + 4+ 4 = 10

plus activation and temp memory which would be the same in either case.

Am I missing something in my understanding of the algorithm?
author: stas00
stas00

body: I'd also drop debug log level eventually as unless I'm missing something it just adds noise - did you see some additional value in debug logs that was useful?
author: stas00

body:
Everything that you write is correct in terms of how 8-bit optimizers work. There can be several issues why you do not see a decrease in memory.

1. PyTorch _does not release memory_ to enable more efficient future allocation by reusing old tensor chunks for allocation. This is why if you track nvidia-smi or allocated memory statistics you will not see a difference. To track the difference you need to look at used tensors (not allocated ones). PyTorch has a function for this.
2. If the weight tensor is below a certain size (I think it was 8192), 32-bit optimizers are used by default and no memory is saved.
author: TimDettmers

body:
> Everything that you write is correct in terms of how 8-bit optimizers work. 

Thank you for validating my notes, Tim.

Could you please confirm whether it staggers the dequantization and quantization cycle during `step` so that only one param is processed at the time, and thus requiring `6x largest param numel` of additional gpu memory bytes, or does it do it for all params in one go and thus require `6x total params numel`?

> There can be several issues why you do not see a decrease in memory.
>  1. PyTorch _does not release memory_ to enable more efficient future allocation by reusing old tensor chunks for allocation. This is why if you track nvidia-smi or allocated memory statistics you will not see a difference. To track the difference you need to look at used tensors (not allocated ones). PyTorch has a function for this.

The memory tracker isn't using `nvidia-smi` (or nvml) but torch memory management API including clearing cache, see:
https://github.com/huggingface/transformers/blob/7c5d79912a21880ce13d77881940458e90d98917/src/transformers/trainer_utils.py#L331

Except of course it doesn't know to run `torch.cuda.empty_cache()` before `step`. I guess I could manually insert it just for the sake of seeing that it works.

What other pytorch function are you referring to? I'm using `torch.cuda.memory_allocated` and not `torch.cuda.memory_reserved`:
https://pytorch.org/docs/stable/notes/cuda.html#memory-management


I will probably need to try to pre-allocate the gpu memory so that only enough remains for it to run `-6 bytes` per param and see if this forces pytorch to reuse the cached memory.
 
> 2. If the weight tensor is below a certain size (I think it was 8192), 32-bit optimizers are used by default and no memory is saved.

Since the allocated and peak memory pairs are very different with and without bnb, I don't think we are hitting this particular case.

Any suggestions at how to write such a test? 

I'm thinking perhaps forcing to run it on `cpu` and measuring peak RSS, but usually cpu memory is even more difficult to measure correctly than gpu memory due to linux's super complicated CPU memory management.
author: stas00

body:
> Could you please confirm whether it staggers the dequantization and quantization cycle during `step` so that only one param is processed at the time, and thus requiring `6x largest param numel` of additional gpu memory bytes, or does it do it for all params in one go and thus require `6x total params numel`?

Oh, this actually works differently. Expansion to 32-bit is done in cache and never on the GPU memory level, as such only the 8-bit tensor exist at the PyTorch level and never any 16/32-bit copy.

> The memory tracker isn't using `nvidia-smi` (or nvml) but torch memory management API including clearing cache, see:
> 
> https://github.com/huggingface/transformers/blob/7c5d79912a21880ce13d77881940458e90d98917/src/transformers/trainer_utils.py#L331

My guess is that some buffers are freed but their memory is not released and then these buffers are reused by the 8-bit optimizer buffers. But even with this there should be some memory decrease.

> Except of course it doesn't know to run `torch.cuda.empty_cache()` before `step`. I guess I could manually insert it just for the sake of seeing that it works.

Unlike standard Adam optimziers, the 8-bit optimizers memory for the buffers is only allocated during the first `step()` call. Might this cause the problem? I this is so, there might be some success to call `torch.cuda.empty_cache()` before the `step()` call. It seems that `empty_cache()` does not release memory chunks which have already been reused, so calling it some time before `step()` might be the right way to do it.

> What other pytorch function are you referring to? I'm using `torch.cuda.memory_allocated` and not `torch.cuda.memory_reserved`: https://pytorch.org/docs/stable/notes/cuda.html#memory-management

I do not know the details. It seems `max_memory_allocated()` + `empty_cache()` is the right way to do it. `memory_reserved()` might be able to help to isolated which tensors are reused (for the 8-bit optimizer states) and which are cached.

> Any suggestions at how to write such a test?

I think if one can track reserved + allocated memory before the first `step()` and after it, one probably can determine what memory is allocated. Probably it is easiest and best, to make use of `empty_cache()`, but as I wrote above, this does not free memory blocks which have been reallocated and these might be partially reused for 8-bit states which might not lower the memory footprint. I think its best to use `empty_cache()` whenever some new dynamic memory is allocated (other than model parameters), so to keep the reserved memory to a minimum, then the memory benefits of 8-bit optimizers should be visible I think. So probably, call `empty_cache()` after `forward()` after `backward()` and then before `step()` in the case there are some other things going on in the background. I think with that it should work.
author: TimDettmers

The current test makes a call to an external HF Trainer application which can't be modified, but perhaps we could use a custom small training loop in a test and measure the gpu memory there, then we will have a full control over gpu memory.

A custom look might be the best way to go.

> > > Could you please confirm whether it staggers the dequantization and quantization cycle during `step` so that only one param is processed at the time, and thus requiring `6x largest param numel` of additional gpu memory bytes, or does it do it for all params in one go and thus require `6x total params numel`?
> > 
> > 
> > Oh, this actually works differently. Expansion to 32-bit is done in cache and never on the GPU memory level, as such only the 8-bit tensor exist at the PyTorch level and never any 16/32-bit copy.
> 
> Sorry, I'm still not having full clarity here
> 
> 1. which "cache" are you referring here? something in CPU memory?
> 2. I understand there is not staggering and BNB dequantizes all of the param states at once, so it needs 8x size of model in bytes allocated somewhere...

Sorry, there are so many caches. What I meant was the dequantization/quantization happens in L1 cache of the GPU. So it does not affect the global GPU memory in DRAM. PyTorch only ever sees 8-bit buffers and nothing else. Here the code snippets:
1. [Dequantization (a simple lookup)](https://github.com/facebookresearch/bitsandbytes/blob/main/csrc/kernels.cu#L1492)
2. [Quantization](https://github.com/facebookresearch/bitsandbytes/blob/main/csrc/kernels.cu#L1549)
