body:
Here what we do is to construct the `jit_inputs `for `torch.jit.trace`, as steps:
(1) get the first batch from the dataloader as the `example_batch`
(2) enumerate each tensor from this `example_batch` and create each `example_tensor`
(3) tuple the `jit_inputs ` and it will be used in `torch.jit.trace`.

Therefore we do not need `self.label_names`, we are enumerating the datasets tensors ( batch: {key : tensor}, not related to labels)

Thanks.

body:
```suggestion
Intel® Extension for PyTorch provides further optimizations in jit mode for Transformers series models. It is highly recommended for users to take advantage of Intel® Extension for PyTorch with jit mode. Some frequently used operator patterns from Transformers models are already supported in Intel® Extension for PyTorch with jit mode fusions. Those fusion patterns like Multi-head-attention fusion, Concat Linear, Linear+Add, Linear+Gelu, Add+LayerNorm fusion and etc. are enabled and perform well. The benefit of the fusion is delivered to users in a transparent fashion. According to the analysis, ~70% of most popular NLP tasks in question-answering, text-classification, and token-classification can get performance benefits with these fusion patterns for both Float32 precision and BFloat16 Mixed precision.
```

Una [configuración](main_classes/configuration) es un conjunto de atributos específicos de un modelo. Cada configuración de modelo tiene atributos diferentes. body:
Just an idea: would it be clearer to use a `device` argument here that is `cpu` by default? 

If I'm not mistaken, that allows you to have a consistent use of a `device` variable without having to hardcode `"cpu"` and `"cuda"`. Of course, you'll want a check that CUDA is available if `device="cuda"`, but this could be checked early in the script and throw a runtime error if not.

If you don't think this makes sense, my other suggestion would be to rename `cuda` to `is_cuda` to clearly indicate that it's a `bool`

body:
Thank you very much for this contribution! I understand that the previous method can be slow. I think we didn't realize it too much because we finally saw few checkpoints that used sentencepiece tokenizers using BPE (I only see ['idb-ita/gilberto-uncased-from-camembert' ](https://huggingface.co/idb-ita/gilberto-uncased-from-camembert)personally) and this step is not done very frequently.

Just out of curiosity, which model do you use that uses a BPE sentencepiece model?

I just think it would be nice to add some comments to explain what is being done. I've left as comments some suggestions :blush: 

Finally, I think this addition deserves a slow test that tests the generation of merge rules from a longer and somewhat adversarial vocabulary (trained on XNLI and without normalization) but I can take care of adding it once your PR is merged.
author: SaulLu

body:
Great work, @younesbelkada!

I have a few questions:

1. is this going to be renamed to bloom?

Specifically, there is a plan to make a distillation of this model, so it won't be just 176b but also probably ~40b with the exact same arch. So it probably shouldn't have the param size in its name.

2. then there is the issue of what to with 13B-en

@sIncerass implemented a new arch many months ago here: https://github.com/huggingface/transformers/pull/14084 - the reason it was never merged is because the licensing process got stalled so this just didn't go anywhere.

Now specifically 13b-en is the exact version of 176B, minus alibi and embed-norm.

And the question is whether we want to update @sIncerass's PR which unfortunately has to be heavily redone since transformers has changed a lot since then. or whether we could use this PR's arch for 13b-en with having 2 config switched to turn alibi/embed-norm on/off - or having a whole new arch.

3. and then there is a plethora of other smaller models trained before 176b and during it. do we want to release any of those and then whether we want to use this arch to use those models - with additional on/off flags where needed.

body:
Thanks for the PR! I have a couple of general comments:

1. Naming: we don't use all caps names for classes anymore, so all BLOOMXxx should become `BloomXxx` (like `BertModel`, `RobertaModel` etc). The `WithLMHead` terminology is not used anymore, so we should have `BloomForCausalLM` instead.

2. Tokenization -> the slow tokenizer should be removed?

3. Tests -> we can merge the two extra files in the modeling and tokenization test files respectively, which would also avoid the change in the table for the test fetcher.

body:
why do you need to create a model if you are not really using it?

But the main reason for not creating it is that it requires much much more memory, especially since you're creating it in fp32 so this will require an additional 176*4=704GB of CPU RAM.

Did you actually try it on the 176B size?

Also remember that all those temporary vars when you `cat` tensors all consume additional memory. So it'll add up very quickly.

I wonder if we should just call into `modeling_utils` final model sharding mechanism directly and never need to create the full model in the conversion script.

(I'm just brainstorming - we can discuss on slack - might be easier)
author: stas00

body: Small question: Can we remove everything that's either `max_positions`, `seq_length`. I think one of the advantages of Alibi, is it's extrapolation capabilities, ie infering on sequences longer then what it was trained on thanks to smart relative positional embeddings. In this sense, I'm not sure what we would want to somehow bound the model to be able to work with X tokens, when it can always go to 2X tokens.

Is this actually needed? From what I understand you're trying to `pad` the keys. This should be doable by a simple `add` when you construct the the `padded_causal_mask`

Something like (not tested):
```python
    padded_causal_mask = (
        attention_mask_bool[:, None, key_length - query_length : key_length, None]
        + attention_mask_bool[:, None, None, :key_length]
        + ~causal_mask[:, :, key_length - query_length : key_length, :key_length]
    ).bool()
```

body:
As I commented here https://github.com/huggingface/transformers/pull/17202#discussion_r883109242 this leads to an inefficient runtime.

I wonder if it'd be much more efficient to do a short finetuning with TP=1 when the 176B model is done.

This needs to be done once and everybody will benefit from it, but if you code to compensate for exactness the slowdown it introduces will impact thousands of users multiplied by thousands of hours.

If it's needed at all. That whole checking for exactness doesn't seem to work when converting from one setup to another, be it hardware, software or topology difference, and very likely shouldn't matter as long as Softmax is matching. Though the small changes can aggregate through the chain of blocks and lead to large changes.

We already went through this with 13B and weren't able to get things exact w/o copying non-transformers code over.

body:
```suggestion
   def forward(self, input, mask, max_positions):
        input_dtype = input.dtype
        input_in_16bit = input_dtype in [torch.float16, torch.bfloat16]
        softmax_dtype = torch.float32 if self.softmax_in_fp32 else input_dtype

        if self.scale is not None:
            input = input * self.scale

        if mask is not None:
            mask = mask.to(input.device)
            causal_mask = torch.tril(torch.ones((max_positions, max_positions), dtype=torch.bool)).view(
                1, 1, max_positions, max_positions
            )
            mask_output, padded_causal_mask = self.mask_func(input, mask, causal_mask) if mask is not None else input
            probs = nn.functional.softmax(mask_output, dim=-1, dtype=softmax_dtype) * (~padded_causal_mask)
        else:
            probs = nn.functional.softmax(input, dim=-1, dtype=softmax_dtype)

        if input_in_16bit and self.softmax_in_fp32:
            probs = probs.to(dtype=input_dtype)

        return probs
```
let's improve readability and also drop `torch.nn` since we use `nn` everywhere consistently.

are you sure the logic is correct here? You're growing `tensor_size` for each key and not resetting it. In parallel you're growing `total_size` and adding to it the ever increasing `tensor_size` - I might be missing something, but could you please check this?

for key, value in tensors.each():

OK so I have tried some generations with tp=1 and tp=4. I have also benchmarked the inference time of a single forward pass with these 2 setups. The conclusions are:
- the generations are exactly the same - ie, the argmax are the same

Timing on 4 forward pass (identical input) - TP=4:

author: patrickvonplaten

body:
perhaps the point of this discussion didn't come across in the first place? it's not about similarity but efficiency.

the `dtype` arg isn't just "convert this back to float" it's about doing the accumulation in fp32.

The inputs are already in bf16 and upcasting them to fp32 doesn't change anything as long as any add and multiply operations are accumulated in fp32.

in other words:

`bf16_1.to(fp32)+bf16_2.to(fp32)`

should produce an identical output to:

`accumulate_in_fp32(bf16_1+bf16_2)`

Same with multiply.

perhaps the simplest approach here is to write a small data test and confirm that softmax produces the same result as with the manual casting? If it doesn't then perhaps there is a problem with `softmax` as the dtype arg was specifically added to make this situation more efficient.
author: stas00

body:
when performance benchmarking is done ideally you want to have enough load for at least a few secs in the final report. Otherwise often the overhead of measurement makes the differences very difficult to see. And you want to measure more than one sequence as a single or a few results don't quite give you the right picture. So multiple rounds and then averaging the results.

> TP=4 does not seem to be that slower than TP=1

If you're measuring the ensemble, of course overall the difference will be small. If you were to measure just the parts that we are discussing and the inputs are realistic and many you should see the difference. Those things just add up when you do more than a single inference on a short input, that's why we bother with making small sections of code faster.

> What scares me is the greatest relative difference

I typically have a hard time making sense of that relative side of results in `assert_close`. May be someone else could answer that one.
author: stas00

body:
So I benchmarked the timings of these operations (manual casting vs optimized casting) it appears that the optimized one is ~2.5 times faster.. (I did 10000 computations on a random `bfloat16` tensor of size `batch_size, n_heads, seq_len, seq_len`. I can also confirm that the results are exactly the same, ie the test `torch.testing.assert_close(atol=0.0, rtol=0.0)` passes.
```
Mean elapsed time - Manual casting : 5.763062109997321e-05 ± 1.8747181396809295e-05
Mean elapsed time - Optimized Softmax : 2.0542944000101217e-05 ± 8.583897912597412e-06
```

As suggested by @stas00 I think that we can safely go for the optimized one, ie let the current commit be as it is !

Here is the script that I am using in case you are interested!
```
import time
import torch

timings_classic_softmax = []
timings_optimized_softmax = []
N_samples = 10000
for i in range(N_samples):
  input_in_bf_16 = torch.randn(64, 16, 7, 7, dtype=torch.bfloat16).cuda()

  start = time.process_time()
  _ = torch.nn.Softmax(dim=-1)(input_in_bf_16.float())
  end = time.process_time()
  timings_classic_softmax.append(end-start)

  start = time.process_time()
  _ = torch.nn.functional.softmax(input_in_bf_16, dim=-1, dtype=torch.float32)
  end = time.process_time()
  timings_optimized_softmax.append(end - start)
```

body:
Added a quick change 

```
import time
import torch

timings_classic_softmax = []
timings_optimized_softmax = []
N_samples = 10000
softmax = torch.nn.Softmax(dim=-1)
softmax_optimized = torch.nn.functional.softmax
for i in range(N_samples):
  input_in_bf_16 = torch.randn(64, 16, 7, 7, dtype=torch.bfloat16).cuda()

  start = time.process_time()
  _ = softmax(input_in_bf_16.float())
  end = time.process_time()
  timings_classic_softmax.append(end-start)

  start = time.process_time()
  _ = softmax_optimized(input_in_bf_16, dim=-1, dtype=torch.float32)
  end = time.process_time()
  timings_optimized_softmax.append(end - start)
```

And the optimized softmax is still faster:

```
Mean elapsed time - Manual casting : 3.107046340015103e-05 ± 1.3641752719914514e-05
Mean elapsed time - Optimized Softmax : 1.9583730099994058e-05 ± 5.503149328108005e-06
```

body:
@younesbelkada, I think you didn't paste all of the code in your benchmark - it's missing the reporting code.

I used `timeit` so it does all the work for me and adjusted it to the following, trying to emulate the more realistic use:

```
import torch

n_cycles, n_repeats = 100, 100

input_in_bf_16 = torch.randn(64, 16, 7, 7, dtype=torch.bfloat16).cuda()
softmax = torch.nn.Softmax(dim=-1)

def explicit_cast1():
    for _ in range(n_cycles): _ = softmax(input_in_bf_16.float())
    torch.cuda.synchronize()

def explicit_cast2():
    for _ in range(n_cycles): _ = torch.nn.functional.softmax(input_in_bf_16.float(), dim=-1)
    torch.cuda.synchronize()

def accumulate():
    for _ in range(n_cycles): _ = torch.nn.functional.softmax(input_in_bf_16, dim=-1, dtype=torch.float32)
    torch.cuda.synchronize()

# 1. timeit
import timeit
print(f'explicit_cast1={timeit.Timer("explicit_cast1()", globals=globals()).timeit(number=n_repeats)}')
print(f'explicit_cast2={timeit.Timer("explicit_cast2()", globals=globals()).timeit(number=n_repeats)}')
print(f'accumulate={timeit.Timer("accumulate()", globals=globals()).timeit(number=n_repeats)}')

# 2. cProfile
import cProfile
cProfile.run("explicit_cast2()", sort=-1)
cProfile.run("accumulate()", sort=-1)
```

I'm actually getting pretty identical results for both functional softmax versions. Probably because the inputs are unrealistically small?

```
explicit_cast1=0.28601728507783264
explicit_cast2=0.26381911896169186
accumulate=0.26107061095535755
```

If you look at the cprofile output you can see that the benchmark is not very clear, since the C kernel isn't being shown - probably `builtins.exec` incorporates it as t reports that it has consumed all the time.

So really here we need to use a pytorch profiler instead. 

Probably the easiest version to use is the one with TB-backend https://pytorch.org/tutorials/intermediate/tensorboard_profiler_tutorial.html

body:
If I understand correctly, it is possible to return multiple processors for models like wav2vec, where all of the following are possible:

```python
from transformers import AutoFeatureExtractor, AutoProcessor, AutoTokenizer

model_id = "facebook/wav2vec2-base-960h"
tok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_id)
feat = AutoFeatureExtractor.from_pretrained(model_id)
proc = AutoProcessor.from_pretrained(model_id)
```

So the current logic is to first try to automatically load the appropriate preprocessor, but if multiple are returned then the user specifies which one in the CLI right?

author: deutschmn

body:
Hmm, think usually the following logic holds in Transformers:
If model is in `AutoProcessor` than the model has both a tokenizer and a feature extractor and in this case the processor should be used. If the model only has either a tokenizer or a feature extractor, then this one should be used.
So, I'd prefer either this kind of logic or to force the user to specify it and remove this extra "auto" logic.

@sgugger what do you think?
author: patrickvonplaten

body:
Thanks for the input @patrickvonplaten ! 

The [current logic](https://github.com/huggingface/transformers/blob/d156898f3b9b2c990e5963f5030a7143d57921a2/src/transformers/onnx/__main__.py#L57) in `transformers.onnx` (on `main`, not this PR) involves checking if `config.model_type` exists in one of  `PROCESSOR_MAPPING_NAMES`, `TOKENIZER_MAPPING_NAMES` or `FEATURE_EXTRACTOR_MAPPING_NAMES`.

This fails for checkpoints like [`superb/wav2vec2-base-superb-ks`](https://huggingface.co/superb/wav2vec2-base-superb-ks) (because it tries to load the tokenizer before the feature extractor), but what we could do is use a try/except block and then get the user to specify which preprocessor to use in the CLI when an exception is raised.
author: lewtun

body: In order, which should prioritize the `AutoProcessor` here.
author: sgugger

body:
Thanks for your input, @patrickvonplaten, @sgugger and @lewtun! 

I adapted the logic now as you've suggested, @patrickvonplaten: If a processor exists, it is returned. Otherwise, if a tokenizer or a feature extractor exists, it is returned. If both a tokenizer and a feature extractor exist, an error is raised. The function returns `None` if no preprocessor is found. The error handling is done in the function, so calling it is more convenient.

I've left the option for the user to specify the processor in there for now. It's probably rarely used but might prove helpful in future. Do you think I should keep it or remove it?
author: deutschmn
